summary: System design mock interview. Ria (interviewer) and Sankit (candidate) design a stock broker platform like Groww or Zerodha. Covers: functional requirements (buy/sell orders with limit and market types, real-time stock price, historical charts as good-to-have), NFRs (buy/sell = highly consistent per CAP theorem; stock price = highly available/eventually consistent), back-of-envelope (100M users, 10% DAU, price checking = 2B/day = 20K QPS peak, orders = 2K QPS), architecture (API gateway, Price Service with SSE to clients, Exchange Gateway Processor subscribing to exchange SSEs, TimescaleDB for price history), SSE vs WebSocket trade-off discussion, two client types (new user needing history + live vs recurring user), Order Management Service with RDBMS, order schema with exchange_order_id, Kafka for order buffering, webhook for exchange callbacks, Redis Pub/Sub for price updates, co-location with exchange, dedicated servers for hedge funds, hybrid load balancing, Kafka backpressure for rate limiting.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Hi Sankit. So let's quickly start your system design round. A couple of details before we actually start. First of all, it's going to be around 45 minutes round. So we will have a discussion first for the first 40 minutes and then for the last five minutes if there are any questions and if you want to deep dive into something more we can discuss that. And apart from it, while you're designing the whole system for whatever problem we pick I'll be asking a couple of clarifying questions in the whole process — so I'll stop you in between asking a couple of questions and if I need some more clarity on your design I'll be doing that as well. So are you clear? Should we get started?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Sure. Yeah, we can definitely get started here.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay. So what we're going to do is today we are going to design a stock broker. So are you aware of any popular stock brokers in India?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: I guess we are referring to something like Groww, Upstock, Zerodha or INDMoney.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Absolutely right. So that is what today's problem is. We have to build a stock broker. Now when I say that — know that a stock broker's responsibility is simply to make sure that the transactions and the buying and the selling which is being initiated by the users is being basically going as a request to the exchange. Now exchange — it could be NSE, it could be BSC. Now you don't have to specifically build the exchange. You can already assume that there is an exchange that is available to you which can basically take in your orders and if they are successful give you those responses. So that is already available. You don't have to build that. We're going to focus more on the stock broker part — right, how internally an application like Groww or Zerodha would actually work, interacting with the clients as well as the stock exchange. Does that make sense?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Got it. Got it. So thanks for the clarification. I believe we can definitely very quickly jot down the functional requirements based on whatever you have actually mentioned here. So technically we want to just focus on designing the stock broker platform, right? We can assume that APIs for stock exchange are with us, stock exchange is given already — we don't have to care about the stock exchange — that means like stock exchange mainly also does the order matching and everything so all of that we don't need to care about. And apart from that, I believe one good functionality to definitely take care of for a stock broker platform something like Groww or Upstock is that users — like whatever are the end users — they should be able to initiate an order, like to buy something and sell something and those orders should be passed on to the stock exchange, right? That should be at least a bare minimum thing — that users should be able to place orders, buy or sell, on the platform.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Mhm. And platform forwards it to the exchange.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Correct. One clarification question that I would like to have here is that an order — buy or sell — can be of type limit order, that means of a specific price. If you want to set that always sell beyond this particular price or buy below this particular price only. Or it can be a market order as well as far as my knowledge goes with respect to stock platforms. So do we want to keep a support on around both like limit order and market order? Or we don't need to care about that?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yes, ideally what we would like is our platform to be able to support both. If we would like to buy something at the market price ideally we should be able to do that but if we do have a limit order that should be supported as well. I think in the API itself we could have some way to take care of.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, cool. So, that makes sense. We can definitely keep an eye on that. Apart from this, I believe one good requirement would be that the user should be able to see the price of the stock as well. Then only they will be able to actually place a buy or sell and make a decision for that. So I believe users should be able to see the stock price in real time.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Correct. Right. And do you want us to have a functionality where users should be able to see historic price — like maybe a chart system where we are able to show how the stock has performed for the last 1 hour, 1 day, 7 days, last 1 month, something like that? Do you think?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: I think we can skip that for now. For the functional requirements I think this looks good. Although it would be good to discuss if we have time we can discuss a bit about this towards the end. But I think for now these functional requirements look good. Okay, this maybe I can add as a good-to-have.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Cool. I believe we have a good set of functional requirements. With that I would like to quickly jump up on the non-functional requirements also so that we can start getting some ideas on the system. I believe that this overall stock brokering platform is going to be kind of like a very hybrid platform. Whenever the user wants to do the buying or selling, I believe that the buying and selling should be done in a very highly consistent manner. When I say highly consistent I mean that I'm doing a trade-off with consistency and availability with respect to CAP theorem — that maybe the user is not able to place an order for a particular stock, that would be still okay — but it should not be like the money has been deducted, they placed a buy order but they don't see that buy order being executed or that buy order is lost. So that's why consistency is something that we should definitely focus on. But on the part of seeing the stock price — this part of the system should be kind of like highly available. Right? User should be always able to see the stock price and the stock movements. There might be a chance that the data that they are seeing is maybe 10 seconds old or 30 seconds old or a minute old and we will be able to push the latest data as soon as we have it, but they should be able to at least see whatever price has been there for the complete day so that they can at least make a decision on how the price is actually moving. So I would say that this stock price part should be highly available and if we can make it as consistent as possible that should be something we should try to look upon. Do you think these two are a good consensus to have?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yes, I think that looks good. Can we also focus a little bit about on how many requests can we also expect considering let's say we have a 100 million users? What kind of traffic we can expect on our stock broker?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, assume that — so I was about to come to this part. So let's do some back of the envelope calculation, right? Assuming that we have total 100 million users and I'm assuming that let's say 10% is our daily active user. If I expect that makes our calculation go to 10 million users per day are going to interact on the platform. So we have two main types of request: one is going to be actually buying and selling and one is going to be actually looking at the stock price. What we can definitely ensure is that looking at the stock price is going to be slightly more traffic-heavy on our platform rather than buying and selling — because a lot of people will be monitoring the stocks but the number of times they will be placing an order will be slightly less. So we can say that the stock price looking part is going to be kind of like read-heavy and buying and selling is going to be not that read-heavy. So overall the platform looks more like a read-heavy platform in a nutshell. So considering that we have 10 million daily active users, and assuming that let's say every user checks the price of at least 10 stocks maybe 20 times a day, this makes our overall number of requests to — 10 million into 10 into 20 — that's going to be 2 into 10 to the power 9, that is approximately 2 billion requests per day. And the peak load — we can assume that the peak load is at least 5x of it — so 5x makes it 10 billion requests per day. And if we want to calculate it on a per second basis then in one day we have about 100,000 seconds. So 2 into 10 to the 9 divided by 10 to the 5 comes out as 2 into 10 to the 4 QPS — 20,000 QPS — during the peak load that we might have to handle. And the number of buys and sells are going to be 10% of that, so 2,000 QPS is something that we should definitely try to handle. Is that a good assumption to actually get started with?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yes, that is actually good. We can go ahead.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, cool. Makes sense. With that let's try to focus on both the flows one by one. Now one thing is for sure that we have a dedicated exchange and we don't need to care much about the exchange part — exchange is going to be something that handles a lot of things for us like matching the order and giving us the order history and everything. We are going to have some end user clients who are going to actually interact with our platform. And definitely one thing that I would like to add — because it's a financial platform, this needs to have very high risk consideration in terms of frauds and everything. So we need to have definitely an API gateway in between that is going to ensure that we have all the requests rate limited, authenticated, any kind of a fraud check or whatever we have to do — there should be an API gateway that should technically help us with that. So that is going to be one component of the system very bare minimum.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Now talking about the fact that the client needs to first of all see the stock data — the user should be able to see the stock price. This is the first requirement that I'm trying to fulfill. Now the stock price is going to be regulated by the exchange and the exchange is going to technically give us the corresponding stock price. So technically what we can have is we can have a service in between — let's say a Price Service. What is the responsibility of this Price Service? The responsibility of this Price Service is going to be one thing: it tries to accumulate the data from the exchange for the current price of a particular stock and then gives that stock price back to the corresponding client. So technically this Price Service has to do a communication to the client and there needs to be some way it gets the communication through the exchange as well. So let's see first of all how the communication between this Price Service and the client is technically going to happen. Now this Price Service — the moment it has the latest data from the exchange about the stock price — it needs to send it to the client and there's a good chance that there will be clients who have already opened the page of the stock and will be expecting the new stock price. So a couple of ways: the client can again and again poll our Price Service but polling will be very heavy on the Price Service — we can definitely discard that. WebSockets are something that is a good two-way communication because then the Price Service will be able to send the data back to the client. But in WebSockets generally we use WebSocket when the client also has to send something to the server and the server has to send something to the client — more of a two-way communication. This doesn't look like a two-way communication to us. What is the client going to do? The client is going to technically subscribe or open up a stock page and expect the stock tick to come up. So I believe a good way would be that there should be some sense of server-sent events coming up altogether. These server-sent events should ensure that the Price Service is actually sending to the client through this API gateway whenever a new stock tick is there.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Sorry to interrupt you in between. I just had a couple of questions. You mentioned that you'll go ahead with server-sent events and you'll not be going ahead with the WebSockets approach. Do you think there are any substantial disadvantages of using WebSockets? Is there a reason why WebSockets won't fit in this scenario at all and server-sent events are something which is more preferable? Why do you think server-sent events are very suiting for this particular scenario?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: See, to be very honest both could have worked. But I believe — first reason why I would not like to go with WebSocket is that WebSocket as an overall connection platform is mainly to ensure or facilitate two-way communication. This doesn't look like a two-way communication. So first thing is WebSocket might be an overkill. Second thing is that let's say the client has opened two or three stocks on the platform — let's say we are giving them a way that they can add multiple stocks and open the charts in parallel. For every chart, if we will maintain a WebSocket connection then the client has to maintain a lot of WebSocket connections to our server. This puts an overhead on the client side and our client application might be overwhelmed with the number of open WebSocket connections. So I believe the number of open WebSocket connections should be kept as few as possible for ensuring the performance. And as of now we don't see a requirement for WebSocket connection because the client doesn't have to send something to the server — maybe the only thing the client has to do is while setting up the persistent connection for SSE, they can just set up which stock symbol they want the latest price for and that is it. The persistent connection can happen and the data can come back. It's just that the client doesn't have to send anything else after the connection has been set up to receive the price. Hence I believe WebSocket is something that we can definitely avoid as of now.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay. And so whenever a person or a client basically opens the stock broker app, you can expect two types of clients. Number one is going to be somebody who's basically trying to look at the stock price for the very first thing in the day — they're opening the app probably after a day or after two or three days. And the second type of client is a recurring user who's constantly on the stock page and consistently trying to see the update of the price or if something has changed or not. So when you talk about SSE, what kind of scenario are we really dealing with over here?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay. So for now SSE is going to technically work for those clients which are let's say already on the page — like your client number two — who are already on the page, they were already looking at the stock price and through SSE the stock price updates are going to come up. For the first client — let's say the stock session has opened at 9:15 and it's as of now let's say 11:00 a.m. morning and now somebody like client one opens a page. So they don't just need the latest stock tick update — they also need the complete day data — how the stock has performed for that particular day. So we are going to add the setup for this flow as well that they are able to see the slight bit of history for that particular day of that particular stock and once they are on the page then the SSE should be established and SSE is going to send them the latest stock tick altogether. Does that answer your question?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yes, you can go ahead.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Cool. Now talking about the two-way communication between the price service — on the server side. Again, price service can maybe do a polling to the exchange but exchanges are platforms which are already having a high amount of load and they have to do a lot of processing. So polling the exchange again and again for a price update is again not going to be the best thing. Ideally what should technically happen is that the exchange should also send us the events for the stock ticks — our price service should try to subscribe. For example I believe on NSE there are 5,000 symbols altogether. So what this Price Service should do is subscribe to a kind of API from the exchange which is going to be driven by server-sent events as well — so once this connection has been established between our Price Service and the exchange, the exchange can keep on sending us the corresponding set of price updates. This puts slightly less load on the exchange because then the exchange will not have to accept any request and process it. Whenever there is a stock price update because of the buys and sell orders and the volume then the update can be sent to the Price Service. And generally for platforms like Groww, Zerodha they have to subscribe and pay some amount of money to this particular exchange in order to subscribe to these events. So the only thing is that with this particular infrastructure we will be able to facilitate the new incoming stock ticks but for a client like client number one who is opening the page in the middle of the day — they also want the historical data for the day. So what we can actually do is we can use single responsibility principle to ensure that the Price Service is only responsible for showing all the prices to the client and because the exchange is more or less an outer world system — what we can have is a service called the Exchange Gateway Processor. What is the responsibility of this Exchange Gateway Processor? First, because this exchange is going to be on the outer world, it's outside of our private network altogether. So this will be responsible for doing any external communication to the exchange. What we can do is the Exchange Gateway Processor is going to actually receive all the server-sent events from the stock exchange like NSE, BSE, and because it has already subscribed it is receiving all of the data for us. What we can have is a storage in between — this Exchange Processor can store that data. So let's say at 9:15 the trading session started. The exchange is going to start sending us the updated stock tick. This Exchange Gateway Processor is going to technically store that data inside this storage. And let's say somebody like client one actually comes up — the client one is going to make a request to show me the last one and a half hour stock price and the latest stock ticks. So for sending the last one and a half hour stock price the Price Service is going to fetch the data from this storage. And apart from this there should be a direct communication between the Exchange Gateway Processor and Price Service — maybe via some API call — that the latest price update that is being fetched from the exchange is going to be sent to the Price Service and the Price Service is going to use SSE to send it to both of the corresponding clients.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Can you talk a bit more about what kind of storage you're going to opt for? That is question number one. And question number two would be: how would you really store the data — in what structure are you going to store the data in this storage?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay. So for this storage I believe we would like this storage to be in a way that all the stock ticks for a particular time stamp — at what particular timestamp and what particular stock symbol we are actually looking at — for that we want to store the stock prices. And although it's not our requirement but we had a good-to-have requirement of old price history charts and everything as well. So if we can have this storage as let's say something like a time series DB — maybe something like TimescaleDB — that can be useful for us because we would like to see the price for the last 1 and a half hour or the last particular day whatever has been there. And apart from that maybe we can shard this storage based on the stock symbols — those stock symbols which have extremely high trading volume, maybe the top 50 or top 100 on Nifty, are in one shard and those which are having lesser amount of volume are on a separate shard. So sharding strategy we can think about but I believe a good time series DB we can actually opt in here for the storage. And how would we really store the data? So for example as far as I know TimescaleDB is based on top of PostgreSQL. So we can have a table prepared that is going to be a price history table and inside this table whenever a price update is going to come up we can mention the timestamp of when the price update came up and what price value we have actually got for one particular symbol. And we can bucket it accordingly. We can have the bucketing done let's say on every 3 hours or every day kind of a thing as well. So the timestamp and whatever price value we have — we can have that — and we can index it with respect to the timestamp. And whenever the user actually wants we can show it in order of the right timestamps, like let's say from 9:15 then let's say next tick came up in 9:17 and so on. So however the price fluctuates, based on that we can send the data to the Price Service and then eventually to the client.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Got it. Okay, yeah, let's go ahead.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So the API should technically look like this: it should be a GET request to something like /api/v1/stock-price and in the query params we can send a symbol — something like let's say INFY — and we should be able to see all the stock price history for Infosys and also the moment we see the stock price history this should also help us to subscribe to that particular stock and any latest stock price update should also be sent via the SSE altogether. This is going to be one API that we can have in order to see the corresponding price history. If we want to have the filters and everything — let's say for last one day or something — so we can have a parameter like timeRange equals to something like '1d' or '1month' or '1hour', these kind of time range values we can pass if we want bucketed data to be sent from our Price Service for historic chart viewing. This is going to be the API that we can have. Now our one requirement is fulfilled — we are able to see the stock price and the good-to-have requirement is also there. Now we should be able to place the orders.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: I believe the order placing responsibility should not go to the Price Service again because of separation of concerns and single responsibility principle. And I believe we also want to do a couple of things — whenever an order comes up we would also like to maintain an order history on our end so that we do not have to again and again request from the exchange what were the last 30 orders for this particular user. This will be a very expensive API to the exchange. So what we can have is a separate mechanism built in — let's call it the Order Management Service, or OMS. What OMS is going to ensure is that any kind of a buy or sell order call that actually comes up from our client is going to be technically entertained by OMS. Now OMS is going to do a couple of things. First, whenever a buy or sell order comes up, what it technically should try to do is it should try to store that in a separate storage — the Order DB. Why are we maintaining an order DB if the exchange also maintains orders? There are a couple of reasons. First, during trading times — in India for example trading is from 9:15 a.m. to 3:30 p.m. — during the trade times the exchange is already bombarded with a lot of requests and for every request we most probably have to pay the exchange as well. So we would like to keep the number of requests coming back from the exchange as low as possible. Second thing: we are maintaining this Order DB to also see a lot of historic data — maybe we can have some kind of OLAP system or analytic platform built on top of this where we can see what has been the user history or user interaction profile. And for any kind of reconciliation later — if any kind of security or privacy aspect comes up — we can also reconcile that okay this particular order at this point of time was actually recorded on our platform. So a lot of benefits we will get from storing this in our Order DB.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: The Order DB's nature should be — we should be able to support good ACID compliance because whenever somebody places an order there might be a lot of operations going on. So either all of those operations should work or none should work. So I believe a good ACID-compliant order database should be there — something like PostgreSQL or some RDBMS. We can have AWS RDS with which we can deploy an RDBMS that can take all the order data altogether. Now the data structure for each order is going to look something like this: we are going to have a unique ID — that is order ID as a primary key. We are going to have let's say the user ID who has actually placed the order. We are going to have the symbol — the stock symbol for which we have placed the order. Maybe the timestamp when the order was placed. And we should have a status of the order — whether the order is in a placed state, pending state, fulfilled state, cancelled state, and so on. Initially the status can be pending. So this should probably be some kind of enum. Now apart from all of this there should be one extra property that we are going to have — that is an exchange order ID. What is this exchange order ID? Because the exchange also maintains a database on their end — so once our Order Management Service is going to technically send the order data to the corresponding exchange, the exchange is also going to store that on their end and there is going to be a unique identifier for that particular order on the exchange side as well. So later for any kind of reconciliation, the exchange will only deal with the unique order ID that the exchange has prepared. So this order ID that I have mentioned — this is our own database's primary key ID. This exchange order ID is going to be the unique ID that is going to be given to us by the exchange. So initially this exchange order ID is going to be null — because as of now we have received the order and we are making an order entry inside the order database.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Once this order entry has been added in the corresponding order database, what we can try to do is after this we can have a streaming system in between — because again we do not want to directly bombard all the orders to the exchange because exchange is already overloaded. So there can be some streaming platform like Kafka. So what OMS should try to do is add the order to Kafka, and then this Exchange Gateway Processor can have one more responsibility — it can take one by one all of the orders and try to put the orders in place to the exchange. And once it places the order to the exchange, the exchange is going to technically give back a corresponding external order ID and that external order ID should then be updated to our Order DB. And again, even the exchange can take some time to process the order. So if we want to get an update about whether the order has been fulfilled — instead of using SSE or polling — I believe exchange should be given some kind of a webhook to our Exchange Gateway Processor that whenever they have an order update they can call this particular webhook and this webhook will update the order status inside the Order DB. This is going to ensure that we are not polling and even for SSE we have to maintain a persistent connection — we can avoid that. Whenever exchange has that order update, exchange can actually give us the corresponding order update through a webhook.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: The order placement API should be a POST request to /api/v1/order and in the request body we are going to send: the symbol of the order, the order type — whether it's a buy order or sell order, and then one more type property which will be limit or market price. If in case it's a limit order then we also need to send at what price we want to place this particular limit order and in case it's a market price then this price property can be null as well. Apart from that, the user ID and everything we can send separately in the header to ensure whatever authentication mechanism we have with the exchange.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Another question: so sometimes what happens in a stock broker is that a client tries to place the order and here in your case it will pass through OMS and then it will be passed to the exchange and let's say it's a limit order — I want to buy at a certain price — but that order never really gets processed. And it will stay in our system. So what your current design does is that if there's an order that's in a pending state — it was accepted by the exchange but eventually it never got placed. How do you want to deal with that data? Will it just sit in the RDBMS because it's going to be in the pending state forever? How are you going to design that part?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Exchange is going to call us whenever they have an update. If let's say we want to ensure that we do not wait for this exchange webhook maybe we can have a background process going on that maybe in a batch of orders takes an update from the exchange that okay these were the orders placed in the last 5 minutes — what are the updates for these orders? There can be a batch call that we can do with the exchange with a separate service which is again and again doing a reconciliation with the exchange. But again this will add more API calls that we are going to make to the corresponding exchange. But what if the order never gets placed? So I was saying that this exchange order ID is going to be something that is going to help us. If the order has been received by the exchange we can have a contract between them and us that if we have received an exchange order ID then the order has been accepted by the exchange. In case we do not have an exchange order ID, what we can use is our streaming platform — the order that was actually picked up by the gateway processor and sent to the exchange but we have not received an exchange order ID for that. So that order we can do a retry for a couple of times and let's say after four or five retries if that doesn't work then we can add that particular order to a dead letter queue and maybe with manual intervention or a separate process we can retry those dead letter queue orders or we can see what went wrong with that particular order. So there can be a mechanism for that. And using this exchange order ID we will be able to ensure whether the exchange has accepted our order or not. If the exchange has accepted our order it's now on the exchange that when they fulfill the order.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, yeah, that sounds good. I have one last question and then if you have any closing remarks for your design you can discuss that with me. So if you will see in your design for Price Service and Exchange Gateway Processor — there could be situations where the price of the stock is changing quite frequently right and with that there's not only one stock price there could be multiple stock prices and that could put a lot of load on your Price Service right and it could be bombarded with a lot of price changes. Do you think having a simple API which is just sending the data updates would be very optimized in this scenario?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yeah, so I was about to take up that because what I see is that we have most of the functional requirements already done. I was about to come to the non-functional requirements — let's see whether the system is able to scale for this much amount of load or not. So I would like to take up all of that one by one. So again because of the fact that we wanted to ensure buy and sell is highly consistent, we have this more transactional-compliant DBMS altogether. Now for making sure that we are able to handle this much amount of scale, I believe we need to do optimization on multiple levels. The first level of optimization — you are saying right that we are bombarding this API again and again to the Price Service and the Exchange Gateway Processor now has a lot of things to do as well. So instead of this what we can maybe try to do is again we could have a streaming platform in between using which this Exchange Gateway Processor can send that data to the streaming platform and Price Service can subscribe to it. But the problem would be — this is definitely a good design — but the problem is that Price Service has to again and again go and pull the corresponding Kafka queue and kind of like consume it. It's more like a pull model from the streaming platform. What would be better is that instead of a pull model we have a push-based mechanism and for that maybe something like a Redis realtime Pub/Sub can actually help us — that whenever we have an update on the price that price update goes to the Redis realtime Pub/Sub and Redis realtime Pub/Sub pushes it to the Price Service. So it will be all in all a push mechanism coming up here and we do not have to bombard our Price Service directly altogether. That is one thing.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Second thing is that again it's not just that we have to handle these many requests. We need to keep the latency as low as possible. So a good strategy in stock broker platforms is that the infrastructure for our complete stock broker platform can be actually placed in the same data center or whatever infrastructure these exchanges are — for example there's a dedicated data center in a particular region where NSE or BSC has deployed their servers. We can try to keep our servers also in the same data center and maybe try to have a contract in a way that we have maybe as much as possible more wired connection directly to the servers. What this is going to ensure is that our overall turnaround time is going to be very less, latency is going to be very less and we will be having much faster order fulfillment and much faster data flowing through us. And one more thing is that even if we are not able to get our servers in the same data center, at least we should try to keep in the same region because there's a good chance that the majority of the load of the stock buying and selling is going to come up from one particular country's region.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Apart from this I believe there should be dedicated servers provisioned for hedge funds and bigger platforms which buy and sell in very high volume rather than the normal retail investors. So I believe there should be dedicated servers pre-provisioned in a warm state for those kind of hedge funds because whenever their request is going to come up their request is going to be in bulk very high value and we need to ensure everything is done for them. So this horizontally-scaled mechanism we might want to have a hybrid strategy here for both of these servers — that we cannot just rely on autoscaling. We would like to have pre-provisioned servers. And sometimes we can also do one thing — let's say one symbol is having very high trading volume. A lot of traffic for one particular symbol can be redirected to one or two dedicated servers. So even the load balancing mechanism we might want to keep hybrid — that instead of just equally distributing all of the load, all the stock data for one particular high volume stock is going through one price server and that server is maybe an extra-large instance on EC2 that is able to take more load. So I believe a good amount of hybrid strategies should be there in order to identify how the load distribution should technically work. And in the Order DB — if we want to ensure that we are able to see past history of orders we should try to do a couple of optimizations. We should try to shard the DB based on user ID so that all one user's orders are going to one shard and every shard maybe we can try to index with respect to the timestamp so that we are able to get the last 30 or 40 orders altogether. So I believe a good sharding strategy should be done here as well.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay Sankit, I think we are out of time. Also, just one last question — maybe try to answer it in a few lines only. Your stock broker platform is very popular and a lot of traffic in a very short amount of time is coming from your stock broker itself. How are you going to limit the ordering? Is that something you will take care of or is that something the exchange should take care of — in case of a very high surge number of orders from a particular stock broker — and how would that work?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So again multiple things can be done. For example I believe the exchange might be already doing a bunch of things around the rate limiting on their aspect first. Second, this Exchange Gateway Processor that we have — we can have dedicated rules done on this Exchange Gateway Processor. If you see the orders that we are submitting, the orders are coming to OMS, from OMS we are putting it into a streaming platform and this Exchange Gateway Processor is one by one picking up the order. So we can try to tune in this platform in order to ensure that we do not bombard our exchange with a lot of order requests. That's why this streaming platform is going to help us — it's going to put a lot of good amount of back pressure. So that is going to be there. Apart from that if we see a surge of price history kind of data then again because of this storage we can try to at least show something to the user and we do not want it to be highly consistent. So we can try to limit the amount of requests that we are sending to the exchange for subscribing to the stocks. We can also ensure that we have high priority obtained for some particular symbols — the top symbols on large cap, midcap, small cap — those are going to be something that we cannot get very stale data on. But there are some symbols for which even if we are getting some stale data we are okay with that. And we can have rules applied for the Exchange Gateway Processor. What I can see is that the maximum amount of surge on these kind of brokerage platforms is going to come up at two times: one when the market session starts and when the market session is about to end. So we can have dedicated server provisioning done for that particular amount of time — that we are going to see a high amount of order load — and at least we can start accepting the order but those orders can be processed by the exchange later. So because of this asynchronous mechanism we will be able to limit the amount of orders.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, I think we're done. Thank you so much for this design. I think it looks good and thank you for the interview.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Cool. Yeah. Thanks. Thanks. Yeah. Thank you for your time.

training_pairs.question: Design a stock broker platform like Groww or Zerodha.
training_pairs.answer: So technically we want to just focus on designing the stock broker platform. We can assume that APIs for the stock exchange are already given — the exchange handles order matching and fulfillment. The bare minimum functional requirement is that users should be able to place buy or sell orders on the platform and the platform forwards it to the exchange. One clarifying question: should we support both limit orders — where you specify a price threshold — and market orders, where you buy at the current market price?
training_pairs.follow_up: Yes, we'd like the platform to support both market and limit orders.

training_pairs.question: What are the functional requirements and non-functional requirements?
training_pairs.answer: Functional requirements: users can place buy or sell orders — both market orders and limit orders — and the platform forwards these to the exchange. Users should be able to see the stock price in real time. Good-to-have: historical price charts showing the last hour, day, week, or month. For non-functional requirements: this is a hybrid platform. For buying and selling: highly consistent — per CAP theorem, I'm trading off availability for consistency. A user not being able to place an order is acceptable, but money being deducted and the order being lost is not. For stock price viewing: highly available and eventually consistent. Users should always be able to see stock movements. Data being 10 to 30 seconds stale is acceptable as long as something is always shown.
training_pairs.follow_up: What kind of traffic can we expect with 100 million users?

training_pairs.question: What kind of traffic can we expect with 100 million users?
training_pairs.answer: Assuming 10% daily active users: 10 million DAU. We have two main request types. Price viewing is read-heavy: 10 million users checking 10 stocks 20 times a day = 2 billion requests per day. At peak load of 5x, that's 10 billion per day. Dividing by roughly 100,000 seconds in a day, peak QPS for price viewing is around 20,000 QPS. Buy/sell orders are approximately 10% of that — about 2,000 QPS. The platform is overall read-heavy.
training_pairs.follow_up: Walk me through the high-level architecture.

training_pairs.question: Walk me through the high-level architecture.
training_pairs.answer: API gateway handles authentication, rate limiting, and fraud checks for all client requests. For real-time price viewing: I'd have a Price Service that accumulates current stock prices from the exchange and pushes them to clients via Server-Sent Events — not WebSockets, because this is a one-directional communication (server to client) and WebSockets would be overkill and put more connection overhead on the client side. I'd also have an Exchange Gateway Processor — this is the component that interfaces with the exchange's external API. It subscribes to the exchange's SSE stream for all stock ticks, stores them in a time series database (TimescaleDB), and pushes updates to the Price Service. For order placement: an Order Management Service receives orders, stores them in a RDBMS (ACID-compliant, e.g., PostgreSQL on AWS RDS), then publishes the order to a Kafka topic. The Exchange Gateway Processor consumes from Kafka, forwards orders one by one to the exchange, receives exchange_order_id back, and updates the Order DB. The exchange calls our webhook when an order status changes.
training_pairs.follow_up: What's the trade-off between SSE and WebSockets for this scenario?

training_pairs.question: What's the trade-off between SSE and WebSockets here? Why not WebSockets?
training_pairs.answer: To be honest both could have worked. But first: WebSocket is mainly for two-way communication — server and client both need to send data. This scenario is primarily server-to-client (the exchange pushing price updates to us, us pushing to the client) — so WebSocket might be an overkill. Second: if the client has opened two or three stocks in parallel, for every chart if we maintain a WebSocket connection the client has to maintain a lot of WebSocket connections to our server. This puts an overhead on the client side and the client application might be overwhelmed with the number of open WebSocket connections. For SSE, while setting up the persistent connection the client can just specify which stock symbol they want the latest price for — and that's it, no need to send anything else after the connection is established. Hence WebSocket is something we can definitely avoid.
training_pairs.follow_up: How does the architecture handle two different client scenarios — a new user opening the app for the first time today versus a recurring user?

training_pairs.question: How does the architecture handle two types of clients — a new user opening the app for the first time that day versus a recurring user already on the page?
training_pairs.answer: For client type two — the recurring user who's already on the stock page — SSE works directly. They're already subscribed and the latest stock ticks come through SSE. For client type one — a new user opening the app at 11 a.m. when trading started at 9:15 — they don't just need the latest tick, they also need the complete day's data to see the chart so far. So the GET /api/v1/stock-price?symbol=INFY API first fetches the day's history from our TimescaleDB storage for that client, and then also establishes an SSE connection for real-time updates going forward. So one API call both returns historical data and starts the SSE subscription.
training_pairs.follow_up: What kind of storage would you use for price history and how would you structure it?

training_pairs.question: What kind of storage would you use for price history and how would you structure it?
training_pairs.answer: I'd use a time series database — something like TimescaleDB, which is based on top of PostgreSQL. The schema would be a price history table with: timestamp of when the price update arrived, the stock symbol, and the price value. We can index by timestamp so queries like 'show me INFY prices for the last 1.5 hours' are efficient. We can also bucket the data by time intervals — every 3 hours or every day. For sharding: shard based on stock symbols — high-volume symbols like the top 50 on Nifty go to one shard, lower-volume symbols to another — to prevent any single shard from being overwhelmed.
training_pairs.follow_up: Walk me through the order management design — OMS, Order DB, and the flow to the exchange.

training_pairs.question: Walk me through the order management design — OMS, Order DB, and the flow to the exchange.
training_pairs.answer: The Order Management Service receives orders from clients through the API gateway. OMS first stores the order in the Order DB — an ACID-compliant RDBMS like PostgreSQL on AWS RDS. Why maintain an Order DB when the exchange also keeps records? Three reasons: minimize expensive exchange API calls during trading hours, enable analytics on user order history, and provide records for reconciliation. Order schema: order_id (PK), user_id, symbol, timestamp, status (enum: pending/fulfilled/cancelled), exchange_order_id (starts as null). After storing in Order DB, OMS publishes the order to a Kafka topic. The Exchange Gateway Processor consumes from Kafka one by one, forwards orders to the exchange, receives exchange_order_id back, and updates that field in the Order DB. For order status updates: rather than polling the exchange or maintaining SSE — since an order can take hours if there's no matching seller — we give the exchange a webhook endpoint. When the exchange has an update, they call our webhook and we update the Order DB.
training_pairs.follow_up: How do you handle orders where the exchange never acknowledges — no exchange_order_id returned?

training_pairs.question: How do you handle orders stuck in pending state — either the exchange never acknowledged or the order never gets matched?
training_pairs.answer: We use the exchange_order_id as the indicator. If we received an exchange_order_id, the exchange accepted the order — it's now on the exchange to fulfill it. If we have not received an exchange_order_id, that means the exchange didn't acknowledge it. We can use Kafka's retry mechanism — retry submitting the order a few times. After four or five failed retries we add that particular order to a dead letter queue for investigation or manual retry. For limit orders that are accepted but never matched — those are correct behavior, they stay in pending and the exchange calls our webhook when they're eventually fulfilled, cancelled, or expired.
training_pairs.follow_up: How would you optimize the price service under high load — many stocks updating frequently?

training_pairs.question: Under high load with many stocks updating frequently, would a simple API approach be optimized? How would you scale?
training_pairs.answer: Instead of the Exchange Gateway Processor directly calling the Price Service via API — which would bombard the Price Service and be a pull model if we used Kafka — I'd use Redis Pub/Sub. The Exchange Gateway Processor publishes price updates to a Redis Pub/Sub channel and the Price Service subscribes and receives pushes. This gives us an all-push mechanism — we don't bombard our Price Service directly. For latency: co-locate our infrastructure in the same data center or at minimum the same region as the exchange servers. NSE and BSC have dedicated data centers. The closer we are, the lower our round-trip latency for order submission and price updates — wired direct connections are even possible. For high-frequency traders like hedge funds: pre-provision dedicated servers in a warm state. Their orders come in bulk and at high value — autoscaling is too slow. For high-volume symbols: hybrid load balancing — route all traffic for one particular high-volume stock to dedicated larger server instances rather than spreading across the pool.
training_pairs.follow_up: How do you control the rate of orders sent to the exchange during traffic surges?

training_pairs.question: How do you control the rate of orders sent to the exchange during traffic surges, and who handles rate limiting — you or the exchange?
training_pairs.answer: Both sides handle it, but we need to handle it on our side too. The exchange likely has its own rate limiting. On our side: the Kafka queue between OMS and the Exchange Gateway Processor acts as natural backpressure — orders queue up and the processor consumes at a controlled rate rather than forwarding all at once. We can tune the number of Exchange Gateway Processor instances and their consumption rate to control the order submission rate. For price data: we can prioritize high-priority symbols — large cap, midcap, small cap — to always have fresh data, while being okay with slightly stale data for lower-volume symbols. We know the two biggest surge windows: market open and market close. We can pre-provision additional capacity on a schedule for those periods. Because of the async mechanism via Kafka, we can accept orders from users immediately and process them with the exchange slightly later, smoothing out the burst.
